chunk_summary: This section discusses common misconceptions in interpreting growth velocity and Z-scores using WHO/IAP charts, emphasizing accurate measurement, longitudinal tracking, and avoiding misclassification errors.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of longitudinal growth monitoring over single-point measurements.
Identify and correct common errors in growth chart interpretation, including centile crossing and measurement inaccuracies.
Recognize the limitations of laboratory markers like serum albumin and prealbumin in assessing nutritional status.
Learn the implications of mixing growth charts and the importance of using age-appropriate tools.
key_facts: Downward centile crossing or slowing height velocity is a stronger indicator of pathology than a low percentile alone.
Measurement errors, such as incorrect age entry or switching between recumbent length and standing height, can lead to false growth interpretations.
Serum albumin and prealbumin are unreliable markers of nutrition status and are better indicators of inflammation or illness.
BMI can be misleading in stunted children, as normal or high BMI does not exclude micronutrient deficiencies or poor diet quality.
Mixing WHO under-5 standards with IAP school-age charts without proper documentation can create apparent centile shifts.
reference_quote: Serum albumin and prealbumin are not reliable measures of nutrition status and are better understood as illness and inflammation markers. (Reference 3)
